utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hey steven so i see that you're here and you've been experiencing some back pain for a while could you tell me a bit about that

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah it's been back has been hurting for a while

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: for about a couple of years and tried a couple things but nothing seems to work

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so let's start start with what happened did something cause the pain

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i think well i played basketball and i bumped one time and i fell and i hit i think i fell on my back got fell real hard

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so it's been hurting after ever since that

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and then how severe is the pain on a scale of like one to ten

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i think it's around a seven

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: wow okay could you describe it for me is it like sharp is it throbbing

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it it hurts pretty bad it's it's sharp

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: throbs sometimes

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and does it radiate

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sometimes it hurts more than others and i'm not sure why

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and does it radiate anywhere

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah it goes down my leg my left leg

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: like my toes get numb a little sometimes

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: wow okay and how about like so hmmm you said it goes down your left leg how about any like are you experiencing any loss of sensation in like the genital or rectal area

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i hope not

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that's okay i i it's gon na help me isolate like what's what exactly is going on so please do n't feel embarrassed to answer the questions do you have any weakness

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah sometimes i try to stand up and and and like i i ca n't go get weak or if i'm standing up for too long

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so would you would you say that there are certain positions that make you feel better or worse

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah like lying on my side makes me feel better

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and then you said that standing also makes it worse right

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah if i stand for a while

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and then have you experienced any like loss of control of like your bladder or your bowels

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i pooped myself once

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so incontinence just once though like over the last two years right it's not like a consistent thing

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no it's not consistent i think i stood up for too long i think i was drinking too

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay you mentioned earlier that you tried some things in the past like what were they

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so i i had a doc gave me some injections before i'm not sure what they were though

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright and i see in your chart here that you had physical therapy did you feel that that was effective

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no i feel like i could do them the same exercises at my house like they charged me and like it

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay but did the did the exercises help

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i mean i guess a little

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay not a so not as effective as we would have liked did they put you on any pain medications

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah it was just a white pill i'm not sure what it was

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay do n't worry i think i can look that up in your chart i just wan na make sure that we have the story straight alright so i know that you said that you mentioned that you played basketball you know before how how like what's your activity like now

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i mean i sit at home all day

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: watch tv

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so not very active i've gained about eighty pounds the past year

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay that's a pretty okay that's a pretty significant weight gain well i i just have a few more questions have you do you know if any of your family members have spine conditions

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i think my fourth cousin has scoliosis

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay but that's that's a little distant but anybody direct like sister brother mom dad

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do n't think so i mean i do n't even my dad though

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay and then do you have a personal history of smoking

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: what type of smoking

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: any smoke you do n't have to tell me exactly but

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: have you smoked

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: next maybe that i have n't

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: are you it's okay i'm your doctor i'm not here to judge you i just want to make sure that i'm like you know when the conclusions that we come to and like my assessment and plan for you i need to know this information so one more time do you smoke or have you smoked before

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay i smoked

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay that's fine how much and how often and what was it

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm not telling you all that i'm just telling you i smoked

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay you have

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: too many questions are you the fit

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i'm your doctor i have to

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: are you the fence you sound like the fence next question

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i'm not a op i promise alright that's fine but let me know are you currently like smoking

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay i just have to ask alright so i do wan na do a quick physical exam i did review your vitals and like part of why i wan na know about your smoking history is that you are stating kind of low like it looks like you are might be in the ninety five percentile range and so we might need to explore some things

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sent them off yeah

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: pardon

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: because i'm fat my cousin was fat too and and he gained a bunch of weight he he had to have oxygen

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you know honestly there are a few other factors weight gain does n't necessarily help you're right but like smoking has like a stronger impact so but it's okay we can explore like you know other options here i as i listen to your heart you have a regular rate and rhythm i do n't appreciate any murmur on so i'm gon na go ahead and do a back exam i do n't notice any overlying erythema or ecchymosis on your skin so no redness or bruising when i push here on your spine does that hurt okay okay so there is midline tenderness on the l4 l5 disk space with right sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness when you bend forward does it hurt

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah it hurts

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and when you bend backward does it hurt

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: a little bit

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so pain with lumbar flexion and extension alright and as i raise your leg does this hurt when i raise your leg up

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: stop

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so supine straight leg test positive alright just a couple more things on neurological examination there is decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh at l4 dermatome patella and achilles reflexes are symmetrical so i just wanted to make sure that i documented all of that in your chart and in a you can ask me any questions but i did look over the results of the

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: what does that mean like am i dying

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: no no no you're not dying but i think we have i think i have a good idea of what's causing your back pain so first let's talk about the mri that you had the results show a disk desiccation a disk bulge with parison paracentral disk herniation resulting in moderate right for neural foraminal stenosis okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that's a big word

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: those are huge words it's okay so let me tell you what like what all this means and what we're gon na do like for my assessment and plan so for your problem of chronic lumbar back pain based on your symptoms your exam and reviewing your mri that's consistent with a herniated disk at the l4 l5 with radiculopathy what this means is that one of your disks that sits between your spinal vertebrae is bulging out and this bulge is pinching on some nerves that's why you feel the tingling in your leg alright so that it's also why you felt pain when i pushed on that area so i'm gon na recommend some a spinal injection with a strong anti-inflammatory medication called a corticosteroid right i think you said that you had one before but i think this is something

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i had to have someone hold me down i do n't like needles so do you can i take a pill i do n't want that

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: we could yeah we could give you something to help calm down like the anxiety i would just write you a prescription for one and you would take that maybe three hours before you came in for your appointment and then you should feel calm enough to be able to do the injection

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: can i get pain pills too

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: can you

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: for pain

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: can you get what now

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: pain pills

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so once i inject the cotocoid steroid in the in your spine it's gon na happen under fluoroscopy right and it's going to be it's a it's just a type of of like x-ray sorry to because i need to be very specific in where i do this injection so i say this because you should n't need anymore pain pills after we do this injection it should help with the inflammation and directly target the bulge to help you out so after this injection give it maybe a day and if you say that you're still experiencing pain then we can talk about maybe giving you pain medication but remember before you said it was n't as effective so i think this steroid shot is the best chance that we have

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'd say it was n't effective

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay sorry i i i saw that they gave you strong pain meds but remember you said the white one did you say it was effective

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i said i said the white ones i said they worked

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay i'm sorry so alright so maybe when let's okay so what we're still gon na do though we're gon na do the injection and then if it still hurts after that then we can explore that pain medication i do n't want you to have to consistently be on pain medication it's not necessarily like a good long term solution if after we monitor this you still are experiencing pain we are gon na try the pain meds however i would maybe recommend surgery

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i do n't want i know

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: right and so this is we're just gon na try everything that we can before you know before we get to that place

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: mm-hmm

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: do you have any questions

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: when it when is it when am i having this this when do you want me to get this injection

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so you're gon na go to the front and schedule it this is a very quick and common procedure it should only take about an hour we will need to do this note like if this if this is successful we would only maybe do this four times a year so whenever at the front desk go ahead and schedule i think that mine pretty available to be able to help you with this alright any other questions

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay thank you

detail_list.summary: The doctor initiates the visit to evaluate the patient's back pain.
The patient reports the back pain has persisted for approximately two years.
The patient reports the pain began after a fall while playing basketball two years ago.
The patient rates the pain as a 7 out of 10.
The patient describes the pain as sharp.
The patient reports the pain is occasionally throbbing.
The patient reports the pain radiates down his left leg.
The patient reports intermittent numbness in his toes.
The doctor asks if the patient has loss of sensation in the genital or rectal area.
The patient denies loss of sensation in the genital or rectal area.
The patient reports weakness when standing for prolonged periods.
The patient reports that lying on his side provides pain relief.
The patient reports that prolonged standing exacerbates his pain.
The patient reports one episode of bowel incontinence in the last two years.
The patient attributes the incontinence to prolonged standing and alcohol consumption.
The patient reports having received a spinal injection in the past.
The patient reports minimal relief from physical therapy.
The patient reports having tried an unidentified pain medication in the past.
The patient reports being inactive and sitting at home watching TV all day.
The patient reports gaining 80 pounds over the last year.
The patient reports a 4th cousin has scoliosis.
The patient admits to a history of smoking.
The patient denies currently smoking.

The doctor performs a physical exam: regular heart rate and rhythm, no murmur, no erythema or ecchymosis on the back, midline tenderness at L4-5, right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness, pain with flexion and extension, and positive supine straight leg raise.
The doctor notes decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh (L4 dermatome) and symmetrical patella and Achilles reflexes.
The doctor reviews the MRI results showing disc desiccation, disc bulge, and paracentral disc herniation with moderate right neural foraminal stenosis.
The doctor assesses the patient with chronic lumbar back pain and L4-5 herniated disc with radiculopathy.
The doctor recommends a spinal corticosteroid injection.
The doctor agrees to prescribe medication for anxiety to be taken before the injection.
The patient asks if he can receive pain medication.
The doctor explains the injection procedure and advises that pain medication will be considered if the patient remains symptomatic after the injection.
The doctor states that if the patient remains symptomatic after the injection, they will explore pain medication, but surgery may be recommended.
The doctor instructs the patient to schedule the injection at the front desk and notes that up to four injections per year can be performed.
